from typing import List
import heapq

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)

        # indices that can never be marked
        need = set(changeIndices)
        if len(need) != n:
            return -1

        # We need to find earliest t such that we can select, for each index i,
        # a distinct second <= t at which we "mark" i, and between those seconds
        # we can decrement nums[i] enough so that nums[i] is 0 on the chosen mark second.
        #
        # Equivalent greedy:
        # Process seconds from 1..m. Maintain for each value v the remaining decrements needed.
        # When second s occurs, we may decide to "mark" that index at time s only if we've
        # already decremented it v times (v = initial nums[i]) earlier.
        #
        # Another view:
        # Marking each index i requires nums[i] decrements, but decrements can be interleaved
        # across indices, one per second, excluding seconds used for marking (no decrement that second).
        #
        # For a fixed t, let k = number of marking operations needed up to time t.
        # Since each index must be marked once and all indices appear in changeIndices,
        # up to time t, we can only mark indices that appear in the first t seconds.
        # We need to check feasibility: can we assign one appearance to each index, and schedule
        # decrements before each assigned appearance such that total decrements <= available non-mark seconds.
        #
        # Standard approach:
        # Greedy over time:
        # - Consider we will perform decrements in some seconds and mark in others.
        # - At time s, if changeIndices[s] == i, we have an "opportunity" to mark i.
        # - We decide to mark i at some opportunity when we can spare enough decrements.
        #
        # Implement with priority queue:
        # For each index i with nums[i] = req, marking it consumes:
        #   - req decrement-units prior to its chosen mark time
        #   - 1 second at the chosen mark time
        # Suppose we choose to mark a set of indices within first t seconds.
        # Total seconds available = t
        # Total decrements needed = sum(req)
        # Total marking seconds = number of indices marked
        # Need sum(req) + count <= t, but also each individual req must fit before its specific chosen second.
        # The greedy works by always marking the index that becomes "ready" earliest.
        #
        # There is known simpler transformation:
        # For each i, define deadline for marking in terms of time:
        # If we mark index i at second s, we must have performed req decrements before s, meaning
        # there are at most s-1 seconds before it. But among those, some are used for other marks.
        # The greedy priority-queue simulation enforces this.
        #
        # We'll simulate from left to right and pick which indices to mark.
        # At second s:
        #   - If index x appears, we can choose to "activate" it for possible marking.
        #   - We maintain a max-heap of candidates ordered by remaining requirement,
        #     and we decide to mark an index when it's necessary to keep feasibility.
        #
        # Practical known solution for this LeetCode problem:
        # Use max-heap of req values seen so far for indices that still need marking,
        # and greedily decide which indices to mark by time t:
        #
        # For each index i, only its first chosen occurrence matters in a feasibility check,
        # so we compute answer by scanning seconds and maintaining chosen indices.
        #
        # The actual correct method:
        # Let positions[s] be changeIndices[s].
        # For each i, there are multiple occurrences; choosing earlier occurrences is always better,
        # because it gives more time for decrements.
        # Therefore, in an optimal schedule, for each marked index i we use its earliest occurrence
        # that is at or after the last time we used for marking/decrements accordingly.
        #
        # We'll instead implement a known efficient method:
        # Binary search t + check(t) with greedy.
        #
        # check(t):
        # Consider only the first t seconds, we can use some of the t occurrences as marking times.
        # We must mark all indices. For each index i, we can mark it at the earliest occurrence <= t
        # among the unused occurrences. But decrements can be scheduled in remaining seconds.
        #
        # Greedy for check(t):
        # - For each index i, collect all occurrence positions <= t.
        # - We will assign each index i to one occurrence position (distinct).
        # - If we assign index i to position p (1-indexed seconds), then we must have completed nums[i] decrements
        #   in the first p-1 seconds, but those p-1 seconds also include decrements and earlier marks.
        #
        # Equivalent global counting:
        # Process assigned mark times in increasing order.
        # Suppose we assign k indices with mark times p1 < p2 < ... < pk (within <= t).
        # Before p_j, there are p_j - 1 seconds total, of which (j-1) are marking seconds.
        # So decrements available before p_j equals (p_j - 1) - (j - 1) = p_j - j.
        # Thus index assigned to p_j must satisfy nums[i] <= p_j - j.
        #
        # So if we sort chosen occurrence positions increasing and greedily assign the largest nums
        # to the largest slack, feasibility reduces to:
        # For each j, after sorting both:
        #   nums_assigned_desc[j] <= slack_desc[j]
        #
        # We can do this by:
        # - For each index i, its earliest occurrence <= t will be enough because giving it a later
        #   occurrence only decreases slack for all assignments. Therefore, we can simply take
        #   the earliest occurrence position of each index within first t seconds.
        # If any index doesn't appear in first t, fail.
        # Then sort these positions ascending as p1..pn (since we need all n indices).
        # Sort corresponding requirements nums for greedy: assign largest requirement to largest slack.
        # Slack for the j-th smallest position p_j is p_j - j.
        # Condition: after sorting requirements ascending against slack ascending:
        #   For all j: nums_sorted[j] <= (p_sorted[j] - (j+1))
        # because j is 0-based.
        #
        # This works because with fixed sorted positions, the best matching is monotone.

        def check(t: int) -> bool:
            first_pos = [-1] * (n + 1)
            for s in range(1, t + 1):
                idx = changeIndices[s - 1]
                if first_pos[idx] == -1:
                    first_pos[idx] = s
                # early break if all have been seen
                # (optional)
            for i in range(1, n + 1):
                if first_pos[i] == -1:
                    return False

            pos = [first_pos[i] for i in range(1, n + 1)]
            pos.sort()  # p1 < p2 < ... < pn
            req = sorted(nums)  # a1 <= a2 <= ... <= an

            for j in range(n):  # 0-based
                slack = pos[j] - (j + 1)
                if req[j] > slack:
                    return False
            return True

        # If even with all m seconds it fails -> -1
        if not check(m):
            return -1

        lo, hi = 1, m
        ans = m
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = (lo + hi) // 2
            if check(mid):
                ans = mid
                hi = mid - 1
            else:
                lo = mid + 1
        return ans

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)

        # But decrements and markings are interleaved; only constraint is that for index i,

        #
        # This becomes: choose for each i a marking second t_i <= T from its occurrences,

        # meaning the block must end by that deadline.

        # This matches classic scheduling for single machine with deadlines and no preemption.
        #
        # We just need to justify that we can always realize this with decrements + markings:
        # A block ending at second d means the marking of that index occurs at d (or earlier),
        # and its decrements occupy earlier seconds in that block. Sequential blocks cover exactly
        # all seconds with no idle. Idling could help fit deadlines only if it reduces completion
        # times, but idling never helps feasibility, so we can assume no idle.
        #
        # Therefore check(T) reduces to:
        #   Let for each index i, choose deadline_i = last occurrence <= T.
        #   Sort indices by deadline_i.
        #   If cumulative sum(nums[i]+1) <= current_deadline for every i in that order, feasible.
        #
        # This greedy is sufficient because it is the optimal feasibility criterion for
        # scheduling non-preemptive jobs on a single machine with deadlines when jobs all start at 0
        # and machine processes one unit per time slot, and each job i has processing time p_i = nums[i]+1.
        #
        # Note: We also require the marking action to happen exactly at some occurrence, not just
        # before deadline. But using the last occurrence <= T ensures there exists an occurrence at or
        # before that deadline; scheduling ends by deadline, so we can align the marking to the chosen
        # second within that window. Because changeIndices provides discrete possible marking times,
        # using last occurrence as deadline ensures at least one exact marking time exists.
        #
        # Let's proceed with this accepted known reduction.

        def feasible(T: int) -> bool:
            deadlines = []
            for i in range(1, n + 1):
                # last occurrence <= T
                arr = pos[i]
                # find rightmost <= T
                lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
                idx = -1
                while lo <= hi:
                    mid = (lo + hi) // 2
                    if arr[mid] <= T:
                        idx = mid
                        lo = mid + 1
                    else:
                        hi = mid - 1
                if idx == -1:
                    return False
                deadlines.append((arr[idx], i))

            # Sort by deadline
            deadlines.sort()
            total = 0
            for d, i in deadlines:
                total += nums[i - 1] + 1
                if total > d:
                    return False
            return True

        if not feasible(m):
            return -1

        # Binary search earliest T
        left, right = 1, m
        ans = m
        while left <= right:
            mid = (left + right) // 2
            if feasible(mid):
                ans = mid
                right = mid - 1
            else:
                left = mid + 1
        return ans

from typing import List

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)

        # Must be able to mark every index at least once

        # by some times within [1..t] while respecting that we can do at most one decrement per second.
        #

        # you can (and should) mark it when its value becomes 0; but if it isn't 0 yet, it can
        # still be marked later at some occurrence. Still, the earliest completion hinges on
        # ability to make them 0 before their last chance within t. We can simplify using earliest
        # possible marking: since we only have m <= 2000, we use a known correct approach:
        #
        # Dynamic feasibility with greedy over seconds:

        #   x has already received nums[x] decrements.

        # second. This classic problem can be solved with binary search on t and greedy:
        #
        # Maintain remaining decrements needed for indices that have appeared.
        # We always decrement the index with the largest remaining need to maximize
        # the chance to finish by t (similar to "minimum operations to satisfy deadlines").
        #
        # Here, marking opportunities occur at each second; at second s, index x=changeIndices[s]
        # can be marked if its remaining need is 0.

        # Since decrement choice doesn't directly depend on s, we can compute feasibility as:
        # For each second s, before considering marking at s, we may have used some decrements.

        # Precompute for each t the maximum number of indices that can be made zero using

        # Marking is an instantaneous check at the same second in addition to "do nothing";
        # i.e., marking does not prevent decrement in that second? The statement says:
        # "In each second ... you can perform one of the following operations:
        # - Choose i and decrement nums[i] by 1.

        # Therefore, within [1..t], the number of seconds spent decrementing plus the number of

        #
        # We can compute feasibility with a greedy that, at each second, decides whether to
        # spend the second marking or decrementing. A correct greedy:
        # - We'll always decrement until some index becomes zero; then when it appears, we mark it.
        #
        # Use binary search + priority queue:
        # For a given t, we look at occurrences up to t.

        # Each time index i appears, we can potentially spend that second marking it (only once

        # Greedy strategy:

        # This matches scheduling with processing time nums[i] (decrements) and unit-time job

        #
        # We'll implement the known correct method for this LeetCode problem (Earliest Second to Mark

        # among indices that have appeared so far; whenever we can mark (when we decide), we mark

        # For each index i, its decrements must be done before its first time we choose to mark it.

        #

        # - Iterate seconds 1..t:
        #   - If current index x appears, and we can afford to mark it now (remaining[x]==0 and x not marked),

        #   - Else decrement the index with the largest remaining need among those that have appeared

        # it's only useful if that index will appear within t. Thus we only care about indices

        #
        # Then we can decrement any eligible unmarked index even if it hasn't appeared yet.
        # It will be in heap once it first becomes eligible by time? Actually eligibility by time is first_pos<=t,
        # independent of current s. So we can consider all eligible indices from start.
        #
        # This makes the greedy hard; we'll switch to a DP-less known solution:
        # Another correct approach:
        # For each t, compute total decrements needed for indices that must be marked by t:
        # that's all indices with first_pos<=t, but marking times are also needed.
        # However, marking can be delayed until their later occurrences <=t, so the exact count of
        # marking seconds is always exactly number of indices (n) by time t when completed.
        # Thus completion by t requires:
        # total_decrements + n <= t.
        # But that's not sufficient because decrements must occur before marks; yet since decrements
        # are always before the moment we finish, and marks consume the remaining seconds, we
        # also must have enough decrements available before the first time we can start marking each index.
        #
        # Given complexity, we use the standard correct method for this problem:
        # Binary search t, and check with greedy using an ordered multiset based on first occurrences.
        #
        # Implementation below is the accepted approach:
        # - We sort indices by first_pos.
        # - We process occurrences up to t, and use a max-heap of nums[i] to decide decrements,
        #   counting how many indices can be zeroed before their first occurrence.
        #
        # Specifically:
        # For each second s in 1..t:
        #   let x = changeIndices[s]
        #   When x appears, if we have enough decrement time accumulated to reduce nums[x] to 0,
        #   we mark it; otherwise we will spend decrement seconds earlier.
        #
        # We can reformulate as: before time s when we mark index x at its occurrence, we must have
        # done nums[x] decrements. Greedy: always use decrement seconds on the largest remaining nums[x]
        # among indices that have appeared but are not marked yet.
        #
        # This yields correct feasibility for this constraints and known pattern.
        """
        # Helper feasibility check for a given t
        def can(t: int) -> bool:
            # Eligible indices are those that will appear by time t
            eligible = [False] * (n + 1)
            last_needed = 0
            for i in range(1, n + 1):
                if first_pos[i] <= t:
                    eligible[i] = True
            if not all(eligible[1:]):
                return False  # some index never appears in [1..t], cannot be fully marked by then

            remaining = [0] + nums[:]  # remaining decrements to bring to zero
            marked = [False] * (n + 1)

            # Max-heap via negative values of remaining among eligible but unmarked indices
            # We'll decrement the index with largest remaining each time we choose to decrement.
            import heapq
            heap = []
            for i in range(1, n + 1):
                if eligible[i]:
                    heapq.heappush(heap, (-remaining[i], i))

            # But if we always decrement from the heap, we might waste decrements on indices
            # whose remaining is already 0. We'll handle by checking remaining in heap pop.

            time = 0
            # We iterate seconds 1..t; at each second decide:
            # if current index can be marked now -> mark (spend second)
            # else -> decrement one eligible unmarked index (spend second)
            for s in range(1, t + 1):
                x = changeIndices[s - 1]
                time = s
                if not marked[x] and remaining[x] == 0:
                    marked[x] = True
                    # marking consumes this second; no decrement
                    continue

                # If not marked now, we must decrement or do nothing.
                # Doing nothing can never help if we still have unmarked eligible indices,
                # because decrements only move remaining toward 0. So we decrement.
                # But we can also do nothing when heap is empty.
                # Ensure there exists at least one unmarked eligible index with remaining>0
                while heap:
                    negv, i = heapq.heappop(heap)
                    if not eligible[i] or marked[i]:
                        continue
                    if remaining[i] != -negv:
                        continue
                    break
                else:
                    # nothing to decrement; if all remaining eligible already marked, fine, else fail
                    return all(marked[i] for i in range(1, n + 1) if eligible[i])

                # Decrement chosen index by 1
                remaining[i] -= 1
                # Push back if still eligible and not marked (could reach 0)
                heapq.heappush(heap, (-remaining[i], i))

            return all(marked[i] for i in range(1, n + 1) if eligible[i])

        # Binary search earliest t
        lo, hi = 1, m
        ans = -1
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = (lo + hi) // 2
            if can(mid):
                ans = mid
                hi = mid - 1
            else:
                lo = mid + 1
        return ans

```python
from typing import List

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)

        # If some index in [1..n] never appears in changeIndices, it's impossible.
        appears = [0] * (n + 1)
        for x in changeIndices:
            appears[x] += 1
        if any(appears[i] == 0 for i in range(1, n + 1)):
            return -1

        # For each index i, we need the number of decrements applied to reach 0
        # before the earliest time we can "mark" i (i.e., before its last occurrence in prefix).
        #
        # At second s, we can choose to decrement any nums[j], and independently
        # for the current s we may mark changeIndices[s] if its current value is 0.
        #
        # Greedy feasibility check:
        # Consider prefix [1..t]. Each index i that appears in this prefix can potentially be
        # marked at its last occurrence <= t, because earlier occurrences don't matter
        # once we can mark later.
        #
        # We can allocate total decrements across indices. To mark all indices by time t,
        # each index i with last occurrence <= t must have been decremented nums[i] times
        # (to reach 0) by that time. Since we have exactly one decrement per second
        # and some seconds may be wasted on marking/doing nothing, the scheduling is captured
        # by the condition:
        #
        # Sort required decrement counts by the time they become "due" (their last occurrence).
        # Then, with k indices requiring a_i decrements, they must fit into the available
        # number of decrement slots before each due time.
        #
        # Available decrement slots up to time due = t are not all seconds, because
        # at each second we "progress" time regardless of decrement; marking consumes 0 time.
        # Therefore we can still decrement in every second and additionally mark when possible.
        # So the only limitation is total number of decrements cannot exceed t.
        #
        # However due-time constraints matter: you can't decrement an index after its last
        # marking opportunity. So for each index i with due time d_i, total decrements for
        # indices with due <= d_i must be <= d_i.
        #
        # We implement this with a binary search on t and a greedy check.

        # compute last occurrence for each index within any prefix via scanning during check
        # We'll precompute positions (1-indexed seconds) for each index.
        pos = [[] for _ in range(n + 1)]
        for s, x in enumerate(changeIndices, start=1):
            pos[x].append(s)

        def feasible(t: int) -> bool:
            due = []
            for i in range(1, n + 1):
                # last occurrence of i within prefix t
                arr = pos[i]
                # find rightmost <= t
                lo, hi = 0, len(arr)
                while lo < hi:
                    mid = (lo + hi) // 2
                    if arr[mid] <= t:
                        lo = mid + 1
                    else:
                        hi = mid
                if lo == 0:
                    return False  # index can't be marked by t
                due_time = arr[lo - 1]
                due.append((due_time, nums[i - 1]))

            # Sort by due time
            due.sort()
            used = 0
            for d, need in due:
                used += need
                if used > d:
                    return False
            return True

        # If all can be marked, answer is some t in [1..m]; otherwise -1.
        # Binary search for smallest feasible t.
        left, right = 1, m
        ans = -1
        while left <= right:
            mid = (left + right) // 2
            if feasible(mid):
                ans = mid
                right = mid - 1
            else:
                left = mid + 1

        return ans

from typing import List

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)

        # If an index never appears in changeIndices, it can never be marked.
        # Marking only happens at seconds where that index is "targeted".
        appears = [0] * (n + 1)
        for x in changeIndices:
            appears[x] = 1
        for i in range(1, n + 1):
            if appears[i] == 0:
                return -1

        # For a given second t, only the multiset of indices that appear within
        # changeIndices[0..t-1] can potentially be marked. But marking of a
        # particular index requires its value to be decremented down to 0
        # beforehand.
        #
        # Greedy check: decide for each index i how many decrements it must receive.
        # Since decrement operations can be applied at any seconds (not limited to those
        # indices), the only thing that matters is whether the total number of available
        # decrement-steps before each marking moment is enough.
        #
        # At second s, if changeIndices[s-1] == i and nums[i] == 0 at that moment,
        # we can mark it. To make that possible, by the time we reach the first time i
        # appears, we must have decremented i exactly nums[i] times (or more; but more
        # than nums[i] is never needed).
        #
        # Because we can choose *any* index to decrement each second, an optimal strategy
        # for feasibility up to time t is:
        #   - For each index i that appears in [1..t], consider the earliest second
        #     when it could be marked (we can wait to mark it until its first appearance
        #     where it is already 0). This is equivalent to requiring that before that
        #     chosen appearance time, we have performed at least nums[i] decrements total
        #     for those indices whose marking happens then.
        #
        # Standard reduction:
        # Let need[i] = original nums[i]. Each decrement reduces one unit of need for
        # some index. We can perform at most (t - k) decrements where k is the number of
        # marking seconds used within first t, because each second is either a decrement
        # or "do nothing" or mark (marking consumes no decrement resource directly,
        # but it happens only at those seconds and can force us to stop decrementing
        # because we only do one operation per second).
        #
        # At each second where we would like to mark some index, that second cannot be
        # used for decrement. Therefore feasibility depends on a scheduling problem.
        #
        # We can solve it by greedy from left to right:
        # Maintain remaining need for indices. At each second s (1..t):
        #   - If changeIndices[s] equals some index i, we *may* mark it at this second
        #     if its remaining need is 0. Otherwise we do nothing.
        #   - For decrementing, we should always decrement the index with the largest
        #     remaining need among those that still need decrements and are scheduled
        #     to appear in the future; but we can simplify further with a known solution:
        #
        # Known approach for this LeetCode problem:
        #   - Build positions list for each index: the seconds it appears (1-indexed seconds).
        #   - For a candidate t, we simulate using a max-heap of "needs" for indices whose
        #     first appearance within t has occurred but not yet "resolved".
        # This is equivalent to computing whether we can allocate enough decrement seconds
        # before each index's occurrences, and the optimal policy is to decrement the
        # index with the largest need whenever we have spare decrement seconds.
        #
        # Implementation:
        # We binary search earliest t and for check(t) we run:
        #   - Count how many indices are available to be marked within t: all indices that appear
        #     at least once in first t. We need to mark every such index at some occurrence <= t.
        #   - Total seconds available for decrements before each marking is reduced by number of
        #     marking seconds already used.
        #
        # We can implement the standard heap simulation:
        #   - For each index i, let pos[i] be sorted list of seconds where changeIndices==i.
        #   - Consider processing seconds s=1..t. When we encounter i at time s, we add i to a set
        #     of "available to mark" candidates only once, at its first appearance time within t.
        #     If by some marking time we have reduced its need to 0, we can mark it at some later
        #     occurrence; but we can always mark it at its earliest occurrence once ready.
        #
        # A robust and accepted simpler method:
        #   - Let all appearances within prefix t be events at seconds 1..t.
        #   - We will decide which indices get decremented at each second to ensure that when an index
        #     is first encountered, it will eventually become 0 before its last required marking.
        #
        # We'll use the canonical solution:
        #   Sort indices by need; simulate the times of their occurrences.
        #   But because n,m<=2000, we can do O(m log n) per check with binary search: ok.

        # Precompute positions within prefix for each index
        pos = [[] for _ in range(n + 1)]
        for s, x in enumerate(changeIndices, start=1):
            pos[x].append(s)

        # For any t, an index i must have at least one occurrence <= t (otherwise impossible to mark it)
        # which we already ensured for full m; still check(t) will handle prefix.

        def can(t: int) -> bool:
            # Track first occurrence time of each index within prefix t
            first = [0] * (n + 1)
            for i in range(1, n + 1):
                # find first pos <= t
                lst = pos[i]
                # linear scan is fine but O(n*m) too big; use pointer with bisect
            # We'll do bisect with built-in since n,m small.
            import bisect
            needed = nums[:]  # remaining needs initial copy

            # Determine indices that appear in prefix t; they must all be marked by <= t.
            idxs = []
            for i in range(1, n + 1):
                if bisect.bisect_left(pos[i], t + 1) < len(pos[i]) and pos[i][bisect.bisect_left(pos[i], 1)] <= t:
                    # But we want just existence of <=t
                    if pos[i] and pos[i][0] <= t:
                        idxs.append(i)
                    # else not possible to mark within prefix t
                else:
                    # If no occurrence <= t, can't mark within prefix.
                    if pos[i] and pos[i][0] > t:
                        return False
                    # if pos[i] empty, would have returned earlier.

            # Build events: at each second s, the index i=changeIndices[s-1] can potentially be marked.
            # Optimal: whenever we have decrements to spare, decrement an index that is currently "unmarked".
            # We can greedily decrement indices in descending need at each second where we are not marking.
            #
            # Equivalent viewpoint:
            # Between seconds, we choose some seconds to use for decrements.
            # If we mark at a second, we must do it for some index whose need is already 0.
            # Since marking does not change needs, we should mark indices as soon as they become 0.
            #
            # We'll simulate choosing decrements whenever possible and mark whenever possible.
            #
            # Use max-heap of indices by remaining need among those that have appeared so far.
            import heapq
            remaining = nums[:]  # 0-indexed
            appeared = [False] * (n + 1)
            unmarked_count = 0

            # For indices that appear in prefix, they must all be marked by end; track unmarked ones.
            required = set()
            for s in range(1, t + 1):
                i = changeIndices[s - 1]
                required.add(i)
            # If any required index doesn't have remaining need >=0 (always), ok.

            for i in required:
                unmarked_count += 1

            maxheap = []  # (-remaining_need, index)

            for s in range(1, t + 1):
                i = changeIndices[s - 1]

                if not appeared[i]:
                    appeared[i] = True
                    # index becomes eligible to be decremented now (we can decrement anytime anyway),
                    # but for marking, it needs to be 0 at some occurrence; having it in heap helps
                    # decide decrements.
                    heapq.heappush(maxheap, (-remaining[i - 1], i))

                # If we can mark at this second (i already 0), do it.
                if i in required and remaining[i - 1] == 0:
                    # mark now
                    unmarked_count -= 1
                    # remove from heap lazily by setting to -inf handled by check at decrement time
                    # We'll mark by updating required set.
                    required.remove(i)
                    continue

                # Otherwise, we may use this second to decrement to make progress.
                # We should decrement some index that is still unmarked and already appeared,
                # picking the one with largest remaining need.
                # If heap empty, we can only do nothing.
                while maxheap:
                    negneed, j = maxheap[0]
                    if j in required and remaining[j - 1] == -negneed:
                        break
                    heapq.heappop(maxheap)

                if maxheap:
                    negneed, j = heapq.heappop(maxheap)
                    if j in required and remaining[j - 1] > 0:
                        remaining[j - 1] -= 1
                    # push back if still in required (may have become 0, still fine)
                    if j in required:
                        heapq.heappush(maxheap, (-remaining[j - 1], j))
                # else: do nothing

                if unmarked_count == 0:
                    return True

            # End of t: all required indices must be marked
            return unmarked_count == 0

        # Quick binary search over [1..m]
        lo, hi = 1, m
        ans = -1
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = (lo + hi) // 2
            # If mid doesn't include all indices (some index first occurrence > mid), can(mid) should fail.
            if can(mid):
                ans = mid
                hi = mid - 1
            else:
                lo = mid + 1
        return ans

from typing import List

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        """
        Approach:
        - Each second s corresponds to an index k = changeIndices[s].
        - At second s we may mark k iff nums[k] has already been decremented to 0
          (decrements only happen one per second).
        - Greedy check for a given time t:
            We process first t seconds in order, and simulate the best strategy:
            always use the current second's decrement opportunity (when needed) and
            otherwise mark when possible.
        - However, since only indices can be decremented and marking requires
          reaching zero, the optimal strategy for a fixed prefix can be done as:
            Use a count of "available decrements" = t - number_of_used_mark_operations.
          That's circular; instead we use a well-known equivalence:
            For each index i, let req = nums[i].
            To mark i at some occurrence of i in the prefix, we must spend req decrements
            on i before that occurrence, one per second total across all indices.
            The greedy scheduling is:
              walk occurrences in time order; maintain a multiset of indices that
              have been "requested" to be decremented until their required decrements
              are done.
          But here we can simplify further due to small constraints (<=2000):
            We binary search answer and for each mid, run DP-like greedy:
            simulate seconds, keeping remaining decrement needs per index and only decrement
            indices that are required and not yet schedulable.
          We'll instead implement the standard greedy that is correct:
            For a prefix t, the question is whether there exists an order of decrements
            (choosing one index per second) so that for every index i that appears at least once
            in the prefix, we can reduce it to 0 by the first time we choose to mark it.
            Since marking needs exact zero, the best is to mark each index at its earliest occurrence.
            Then each index i must have been fully decremented within the time window before its
            first marking occurrence.
            So we check feasibility using a cumulative decrements constraint:
              For each i, define first occurrence time pos[i] (1..t). If not present => impossible.
              By time pos[i]-1, we must have performed at least nums[i] decrements in total
              over i's requiring work before their deadline.
            This becomes: can we schedule jobs with processing times nums[i] and deadlines
            pos[i]-1, where at most one job can be processed per unit time (preemptions allowed)?
            Preemption is allowed because we can decrement any index each second arbitrarily,
            so we just need total processing before each deadline when using EDF ordering.
            Feasibility with preemption reduces to checking:
              for all deadlines D: sum of processing times of jobs with deadline <= D <= D
            Using the fact that processing can be split across time, EDF works.
            Since we have no upper bound on per-second decrement target, preemption is indeed allowed.
            We'll implement this check:
              - Compute earliest occurrence time pos[i] within prefix t.
              - If some i never occurs in changeIndices[1..t], cannot mark all.
              - Let deadlines d_i = pos[i]-1.
              - Sort jobs by deadline, compute prefix sums of nums[i], and ensure for every k:
                sum_{first k} nums[i] <= d_sorted[k]
        Complexity: O(n log n) per check, with binary search over m (<=2000).
        """
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)

        # Quick impossibility: every index must appear at least once in the whole list
        # else it's impossible to ever mark it.
        total_pos = [m + 1] * n
        for s, idx in enumerate(changeIndices, start=1):
            i = idx - 1
            if total_pos[i] == m + 1:
                total_pos[i] = s
        if any(total_pos[i] == m + 1 for i in range(n)):
            return -1

        # Precompute for prefix checks:
        # We'll compute first occurrence positions within a prefix t by scanning up to t each time.
        # With n,m <= 2000, this is fine: O(m^2).
        # But we can do O(m*n) worst-case still fine.
        # We'll do scanning per check in O(t + n) <= O(m+n).
        def feasible(t: int) -> bool:
            # earliest occurrence within first t seconds
            pos = [m + 1] * n
            for s in range(1, t + 1):
                i = changeIndices[s - 1] - 1
                if pos[i] == m + 1:
                    pos[i] = s
                    # early break if all found
                    # (optional, but helpful)
                    # count found
            for i in range(n):
                if pos[i] == m + 1:
                    return False

            jobs = []
            for i in range(n):
                deadline = pos[i] - 1  # must finish decrements by then
                jobs.append((deadline, nums[i]))
            jobs.sort()  # by deadline

            total = 0
            for deadline, req in jobs:
                total += req
                if total > deadline:
                    return False
            return True

        # Binary search earliest t
        lo, hi = 1, m
        ans = -1
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = (lo + hi) // 2
            if feasible(mid):
                ans = mid
                hi = mid - 1
            else:
                lo = mid + 1
        return ans

from typing import List
from collections import defaultdict

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)

        # Quick impossibility: every index must appear in changeIndices at least once
        last_pos = [-1] * (n + 1)  # 1-indexed
        for s, idx in enumerate(changeIndices, start=1):
            last_pos[idx] = s

        for i in range(1, n + 1):
            if last_pos[i] == -1:
                return -1

        # Map each index i to its required number of decrements to reach 0
        need = [0] * (n + 1)  # 1-indexed
        for i in range(1, n + 1):
            need[i] = nums[i - 1]

        # Binary search the earliest second T such that all indices can be marked by time T
        # Feasibility uses "we can spend at most T operations on decrements" but decrements
        # can be distributed arbitrarily across seconds before their corresponding marks.
        #
        # Key observation:
        # For any prefix of seconds [1..T], only indices whose last_pos <= T are relevant,
        # and each such index i needs need[i] decrements to be marked by its first time
        # we choose to mark it within [1..T]. Greedy feasibility becomes:
        # Process seconds in increasing order; when we reach a time s where we decide to
        # mark index i, we must have already allocated need[i] decrements for it.
        # With the ability to decrement any index at any time, the classic solution is:
        # sort indices by last_pos and keep cumulative required decrements.
        #
        # In prefix up to time T, each index i with last_pos[i] <= T must be decremented
        # down to 0 by then, consuming need[i] operations total across all indices.
        #
        # But note: there is one "mark" operation per second at most, and seconds between
        # decrements are not separate—each second is either a decrement or a do-nothing,
        # and marking can occur in the same second as "do-nothing" (mark attempt happens
        # automatically after deciding operation). In our model, decrements consume seconds,
        # while marks do not consume extra seconds beyond the second when they are eligible.
        #
        # Therefore, to finish all marks by time T, we need:
        # - Total decrements allocated across all indices <= T
        # - Moreover, each index i can only be marked on or before its last_pos[i],
        #   which effectively enforces the "prefix" constraints:
        #   For every k, sum(need[i] for i with last_pos[i] <= t_k) <= t_k,
        #   where t_k are distinct times in increasing order.
        #
        # This is exactly checked by sorting indices by last_pos and verifying prefix sums.

        indices = list(range(1, n + 1))
        indices.sort(key=lambda i: last_pos[i])

        prefix_sum = 0
        # We'll do feasibility checks with binary search using same logic, recomputing prefix each time.
        # n <= 2000, m <= 2000 => O(n log m * n) acceptable, but we can do O(n) per check.

        def feasible(T: int) -> bool:
            # need all indices with last_pos <= T to have been decremented by then
            total = 0
            # indices are sorted by last_pos
            for i in indices:
                if last_pos[i] > T:
                    break
                total += need[i]
                if total > T:
                    return False
            # Also, indices with last_pos > T can't be marked by time T, but we only need
            # all indices marked, so T must be >= max last_pos (otherwise impossible).
            # This is already implied by prefix check and the final check below.
            for i in indices:
                if last_pos[i] > T:
                    return False
            return True

        lo, hi = 1, m
        ans = -1
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = (lo + hi) // 2
            if feasible(mid):
                ans = mid
                hi = mid - 1
            else:
                lo = mid + 1

        return ans

from typing import List

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)
        last_pos = [-1] * n

        # last second in [1..m] when each index appears in changeIndices
        for s, idx in enumerate(changeIndices, start=1):
            last_pos[idx - 1] = s

        # If some index never appears, impossible
        if any(p == -1 for p in last_pos):
            return -1

        # For feasibility by second T, each index i must have been decremented at least
        # nums[i] times by or before its last_pos[i]-th second (since only then we can mark it).
        # Let deadline d_i = last_pos[i] - 1 seconds available for decrements of nums[i].
        deadlines = [(last_pos[i] - 1, nums[i]) for i in range(n)]

        # If deadline < 0, cannot decrement before first marking; only possible if nums[i]==0
        for d, need in deadlines:
            if d < 0 and need > 0:
                return -1

        # Classic scheduling/greedy:
        # Sort by deadline. Maintain how many decrements we can do by each deadline.
        # We have at most d+1 seconds? Actually decrements happen one per second, and marking operations
        # take a full second too, so decrements available before second T are exactly T-1 seconds.
        #
        # Since marking can be done at the same second as checks, the total number of decrement operations
        # performed in seconds <= t is at most t (but we are free to do decrements or do-nothing/mark).
        # For any index i with deadline last_pos[i]-1, its required decrements must be scheduled
        # within first deadline seconds.
        #
        # Greedy check reduces to ensuring: for all k after sorting by deadline,
        # total required decrements of the subset with deadlines <= current deadline <= current deadline.
        #
        # We'll instead build the maximal required multiset using priority queue:
        # Iterate deadlines in increasing order, and keep the largest "need" tasks? But tasks are
        # divisible? No. Each index i requires exactly nums[i] unit time decrements, so it's
        # continuous unit-time processing; then feasibility is simply total work <= available time
        # for all prefixes when processing single machine non-preemptive with fixed processing times.
        # With unit-time and preemption? Decrements are identical and can be interleaved.
        # Since we just need total decrements by each deadline, preemption not necessary because work can
        # be split; thus only total work matters.
        #
        # Therefore: for sorted deadlines, prefix sum of needs <= prefix deadline total time.
        deadlines.sort()  # by deadline
        pref = 0
        for d, need in deadlines:
            pref += need
            if pref > d:
                # Not enough time before this deadline
                return -1

        # Now need earliest second T (1..m) such that all indices can be marked by then.
        # If all indices are feasible with respect to their own last_pos, then answer is max last_pos
        # under the optimal schedule; but marking earlier might be possible if we choose a second <= m.
        #
        # We can binary search earliest T and check feasibility using only indices with last_pos <= T.
        # For indices whose last_pos > T, they haven't been allowed to be marked yet, so the deadline
        # would exceed T-1, but they also can be marked later; however "by second T" all must be marked,
        # so each index i must be markable by T, meaning T >= last_pos[i]. Hence earliest T is:
        # min T such that T >= all last_pos[i] -> T = max last_pos[i], but only if decrements can be done
        # by T-1.
        #
        # Indeed, to have every index marked by T, we must have scheduled marking for each i at some
        # occurrence <= T, which is possible only if T >= last_pos[i]. Thus minimal T is fixed:
        ans = max(last_pos)
        # We also must ensure decrements can be performed by ans-1 for all indices, i.e. total required
        # work for all indices <= ans-1 because by time ans-1 we can do at most ans-1 decrements.
        total = sum(nums)
        if total > ans - 1:
            return -1

        return ans

from typing import List
import heapq

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)

        # Build positions where each index appears in changeIndices
        pos = [[] for _ in range(n + 1)]  # 1-indexed for convenience
        for s, idx in enumerate(changeIndices, start=1):
            pos[idx].append(s)

        # If some nums index is never in changeIndices, impossible
        for i in range(1, n + 1):
            if not pos[i]:
                return -1

        # If an index i has to wait for its k-th occurrence, the deadline will be pos[i][k-1]
        # Need nums[i] decrements before the last required mark.
        # Convert the problem to: choose an assignment of decrements to indices so that each index
        # i with value nums[i] can be marked on some occurrence, and that occurrence must be >=
        # the number of decrements needed up to that time.
        #
        # More direct greedy:
        # Consider any second s as a candidate answer. By time s, each index i that appears
        # in [1..s] can be marked only at its t-th appearance where t <= count_i(s).
        # Marking i requires nums[i] decrements, which consume one decrement operation per second.
        #
        # Standard approach:
        # Define for each index i, among occurrences up to some time, the number of decrements
        # it can cover to be markable by time s must satisfy:
        # nums[i] <= (count_i(s) - 1)?? Actually to mark at some occurrence, you need to have
        # completed nums[i] decrement operations before that marking moment. Since marking moments
        # are fixed at occurrences, and in each second you can either decrement or mark, the
        # total number of decrement operations performed before second s is s - (number of marks done).
        #
        # We use known reduction:
        # Process seconds in order. Maintain how many decrement operations have been used so far,
        # and attempt to mark indices greedily when possible.
        #
        # Equivalent solution:
        # We need to find earliest s such that there exists an order of actions where each index i
        # is marked at some occurrence <= s after performing nums[i] decrements total before each marking.
        #
        # This can be solved by computing "deadlines" for each decrement slot using min-heap:
        # For each index i, if it appears at positions pos[i][0..k-1], then to mark i at
        # its j-th appearance (0-based), the required number of decrements is nums[i] and must
        # be done in the seconds before that occurrence, allowing for other marks.
        # Greedy feasibility is achieved by assigning indices to the latest possible
        # appearance by which they can be marked given a running "decrement budget".
        #
        # We can implement feasibility with incremental simulation:
        # Let need[i]=nums[i-1]. Each time we choose to decrement, it reduces some index's need by 1.
        # We can only decrement one per second. We can mark an index at its occurrence if its need is 0.
        #
        # So for earliest s, simplest is to binary search s and check if possible by time s.
        # But n,m <= 2000 so O(m log n * log m) is fine.
        #
        # We'll do binary search on s with a greedy check:
        # Simulate seconds 1..s. At each second t:
        # - If changeIndices[t] points to index x and need[x]==0, we should mark it (consume no decrement).
        # - Otherwise, we should decrement some index with need>0 (consume one decrement).
        #
        # To maximize chances, decrement indices with the earliest next occurrence (the soonest they can be marked).
        # So we maintain a min-heap keyed by next occurrence time.
        #
        # Precompute for each index i, an iterator over its occurrences and be able to query next occurrence
        # after current time.
        def feasible(s: int) -> bool:
            need = nums[:]  # 0..n-1
            marked = [False] * n

            # For each index, keep pointer to current occurrence position within [1..s]
            ptr = [0] * (n + 1)

            # Collect initial indices that have need>0 (or even need==0 but not marked yet) in heap.
            # Heap entries: (next_occurrence_time, index_i_1based)
            heap = []
            for i in range(1, n + 1):
                if pos[i][0] <= s:
                    nxt = pos[i][0]
                    heap.append((nxt, i))
            heapq.heapify(heap)

            for t in range(1, s + 1):
                x = changeIndices[t - 1]

                # Mark if possible
                if not marked[x] and need[x - 1] == 0:

                    # Advance pointer for x beyond this time, because its next marking chance changes
                    # (it matters only for heap scheduling if need[x] were >0, but once marked,
                    # it won't be decremented anyway).
                    continue

                # Otherwise, we need to use the second to decrement some index to eventually make it markable.
                # If all indices already marked, we could do nothing, but then marking must already be complete.
                if all(marked):
                    return True

                # Choose an index with remaining need>0 whose next occurrence is earliest and not yet passed.
                # Pop until find valid candidate.
                while heap:
                    nxt, i = heap[0]
                    if nxt > s:
                        break  # no remaining occurrence in range
                    if marked[i] or need[i - 1] == 0:
                        heapq.heappop(heap)
                        continue
                    # Valid candidate
                    break

                if not heap:
                    return False

                nxt, i = heap[0]
                if nxt > s:
                    return False

                # Do decrement on i
                heapq.heappop(heap)
                need[i - 1] -= 1

                # Push back if still not marked and still has remaining need>0
                if not marked[i] and need[i - 1] > 0:
                    # Determine its next occurrence after current time t.
                    # Move ptr[i] while occurrences are <= t
                    while ptr[i] < len(pos[i]) and pos[i][ptr[i]] <= t:
                        ptr[i] += 1
                    if ptr[i] < len(pos[i]):
                        next_time = pos[i][ptr[i]]
                        if next_time <= s:
                            heapq.heappush(heap, (next_time, i))
                # else if need becomes 0, we don't decrement it further; it can be marked at its next occurrence.
                # For correctness, we still might need to keep it in heap as candidate for marking scheduling,
                # but marking is handled when its occurrence arrives and need==0.
                # So we don't need it in heap.

            return all(marked)

        # Binary search minimal s
        lo, hi = 1, m
        ans = -1
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = (lo + hi) // 2
            if feasible(mid):
                ans = mid
                hi = mid - 1
            else:
                lo = mid + 1
        return ans
```
```

Before solving, consider these reusable insights from prior analysis:

  1. Constraint-First Prefix Feasibility: Convert the sequential marking/decrement process into an explicit feasibility check for a fixed time horizon T.
     Instead of simulating “earliest” decisions, define what it means to succeed by time T: each index must be marked at some allowed occurrence within [1..T] and all required decrements must fit into the non-marking seconds before those occurrences. Then build a monotone predicate “feasible(T)” and use it with binary search to get the earliest T.
  2. Correctly Account for Consumed Time Slots: When two actions compete for the same per-second resource, always subtract the exact number of seconds spent on the non-resource action (marking) when computing available capacity for the other (decrement).
     A common failure mode is off-by-one or miscounting whether decrement capacity is measured in seconds strictly before a chosen mark, or including/excluding the mark second. Derive the capacity formula from the action schedule (e.g., “available decrement seconds in a prefix = prefix length − number of marking seconds in that prefix”) and apply it consistently in prefix inequalities.
  3. Justify “Latest/Best” Choice with Monotonic Deadlines: If you choose a canonical occurrence time per index (often the latest feasible within T), prove that this choice only relaxes constraints for others.
     Successful solutions rely on the idea that marking later increases available time for decrements, so a “latest occurrence” assignment can be used to derive the least restrictive deadlines. Unsuccessful solutions often assume earliest or last occurrence choices are optimal without proving how the shared decrement-time budget interacts across indices.
  4. Prefer Deadline/Prefix-Sum Checks Over Unproven Greedy Simulations: When greedy simulation decisions are hard to prove optimal, reduce feasibility to a deadline scheduling or prefix-capacity inequality that can be checked deterministically.
     Attempts that decrement “the largest remaining requirement” at each step may look plausible but can be wrong unless there is a formal exchange argument. Deadline-based feasibility (e.g., sorting by deadlines and checking cumulative required work against cumulative available capacity in every prefix) is more robust and easier to validate.
  5. Binary Search Requires Monotonic Feasibility: Before using binary search, ensure the feasibility predicate is monotone with respect to T.
     In these problems, if all indices can be marked by time T, then they can also be marked by any later time T’ because you can follow the same schedule and ignore extra seconds. This monotonicity allows binary search, but only if your feasibility check correctly models the constraints within [1..T].

Apply these insights to the problem below.

### Question:
You are given two 1-indexed integer arrays, nums and, changeIndices, having lengths n and m, respectively.
Initially, all indices in nums are unmarked. Your task is to mark all indices in nums.
In each second, s, in order from 1 to m (inclusive), you can perform one of the following operations:

Choose an index i in the range [1, n] and decrement nums[i] by 1.
If nums[changeIndices[s]] is equal to 0, mark the index changeIndices[s].
Do nothing.

Return an integer denoting the earliest second in the range [1, m] when all indices in nums can be marked by choosing operations optimally, or -1 if it is impossible.
 
Example 1:

Input: nums = [2,2,0], changeIndices = [2,2,2,2,3,2,2,1]
Output: 8
Explanation: In this example, we have 8 seconds. The following operations can be performed to mark all indices:
Second 1: Choose index 1 and decrement nums[1] by one. nums becomes [1,2,0].
Second 2: Choose index 1 and decrement nums[1] by one. nums becomes [0,2,0].
Second 3: Choose index 2 and decrement nums[2] by one. nums becomes [0,1,0].
Second 4: Choose index 2 and decrement nums[2] by one. nums becomes [0,0,0].
Second 5: Mark the index changeIndices[5], which is marking index 3, since nums[3] is equal to 0.
Second 6: Mark the index changeIndices[6], which is marking index 2, since nums[2] is equal to 0.
Second 7: Do nothing.
Second 8: Mark the index changeIndices[8], which is marking index 1, since nums[1] is equal to 0.
Now all indices have been marked.
It can be shown that it is not possible to mark all indices earlier than the 8th second.
Hence, the answer is 8.

Example 2:

Input: nums = [1,3], changeIndices = [1,1,1,2,1,1,1]
Output: 6
Explanation: In this example, we have 7 seconds. The following operations can be performed to mark all indices:
Second 1: Choose index 2 and decrement nums[2] by one. nums becomes [1,2].
Second 2: Choose index 2 and decrement nums[2] by one. nums becomes [1,1].
Second 3: Choose index 2 and decrement nums[2] by one. nums becomes [1,0].
Second 4: Mark the index changeIndices[4], which is marking index 2, since nums[2] is equal to 0.
Second 5: Choose index 1 and decrement nums[1] by one. nums becomes [0,0].
Second 6: Mark the index changeIndices[6], which is marking index 1, since nums[1] is equal to 0.
Now all indices have been marked.
It can be shown that it is not possible to mark all indices earlier than the 6th second.
Hence, the answer is 6.

Example 3:

Input: nums = [0,1], changeIndices = [2,2,2]
Output: -1
Explanation: In this example, it is impossible to mark all indices because index 1 isn't in changeIndices.
Hence, the answer is -1.

 
Constraints:

1 <= n == nums.length <= 2000
0 <= nums[i] <= 10^9
1 <= m == changeIndices.length <= 2000
1 <= changeIndices[i] <= n

    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:

from typing import List
from bisect import bisect_left

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)

        # If some index never appears in changeIndices, it can never be marked.
        need_present = [False] * n
        for x in changeIndices:
            need_present[x - 1] = True
        for i in range(n):
            if not need_present[i]:
                return -1

        # For each index i, it can only be marked at times where changeIndices[t] == i.
        # If nums[i] = k, we must schedule at least k decrements on i before its marking time.
        # Using feasibility(T): for each index i we choose the latest occurrence <= T
        # (this is never worse because it gives more time for decrements).
        # Then we need to check if we can allocate all decrements within the available
        # non-marking seconds before those chosen marking times.
        #
        # Feasibility check reduces to:
        # Let mark_time[i] be the latest t<=T with changeIndices[t]==i. (1-indexed times)
        # Sort indices by mark_time. For a prefix of those indices, by their marking times,
        # we have exactly (mark_time - 1) seconds total before the last one. Within those seconds,
        # we must "reserve" one second for each earlier marking (because those marking seconds
        # happen too and cannot be used for decrements). Thus for any prefix up to a given
        # sorted position with times t_j, the total decrements required by those indices
        # must be <= (#seconds before t_j) - (#markings before or at that time).
        #
        # Careful counting: if an index has mark time t, its marking consumes second t.
        # Decrements for that index must occur strictly before t, so they live in [1..t-1].
        # For a prefix containing k indices with marking times <= t (in sorted order),
        # the number of decrements slots in [1..t-1] that are not used for markings
        # is (t-1) - (k-1) = t-k, because among the k indices, exactly (k-1) have
        # marking times < t and thus consume (k-1) seconds within [1..t-1].
        #
        # This yields the inequality for each prefix:
        # sum_required_prefix <= t_j - k
        #
        # where k is prefix size and t_j is the j-th smallest mark time.
        #
        # We can compute mark_time for each index for each T in O(n) by precomputing
        # occurrence positions and using last <=T. With n,m<=2000, O(m*n log m) is fine,
        # but we'll do it in O(m*n) using one pass per T for simplicity.

        # Precompute positions for each value to speed up last occurrence lookup.
        positions = [[] for _ in range(n)]
        for t, val in enumerate(changeIndices, start=1):
            positions[val - 1].append(t)

        def feasible(T: int) -> bool:
            # If T is too small such that some index's latest occurrence <=T doesn't exist => impossible.
            mark_times = []
            for i in range(n):
                if nums[i] == 0:
                    # It still needs to be marked, but it requires 0 decrements.
                    # It can be marked at latest occurrence <=T like others.
                    pass
                lst = positions[i]
                # Find last position <= T
                # manual binary search
                lo, hi = 0, len(lst)
                while lo < hi:
                    mid = (lo + hi) // 2
                    if lst[mid] <= T:
                        lo = mid + 1
                    else:
                        hi = mid
                idx = lo - 1
                if idx < 0:
                    return False
                mark_times.append(lst[idx])

            # Now apply prefix inequality after sorting by mark time.
            order = sorted(range(n), key=lambda i: mark_times[i])
            prefix_sum = 0
            for k, i in enumerate(order, start=1):
                prefix_sum += nums[i]
                t = mark_times[i]
                # Need prefix_sum <= t - k
                # (t-1) total time before t, minus (k-1) marking seconds before t => t-k slots for decrements.
                if prefix_sum > t - k:
                    return False
            return True

        # Binary search earliest feasible T in [1..m]
        lo, hi = 1, m
        ans = -1
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = (lo + hi) // 2
            if feasible(mid):
                ans = mid
                hi = mid - 1
            else:
                lo = mid + 1
        return ans

from typing import List

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)

        need = nums[:]  # decrements required for each index
        # If an index never appears in changeIndices, impossible
        present = [False] * (n + 1)
        for x in changeIndices:
            present[x] = True
        if any(not present[i] for i in range(1, n + 1)):
            return -1

        # Feasibility by time T:
        # For each index i, we must choose one occurrence position pos_i <= T.
        # Let k(t) be number of marking slots among first t seconds.
        # Then total decrement actions must fit in the remaining seconds:
        #   sum_i need[i] <= (T - k(T))  (but k depends on which occurrences chosen)
        #
        # The "best" (least restrictive) choice is to mark each index at its latest
        # occurrence <= T. That maximizes available time for decrements, and yields
        # canonical deadlines. This makes feasibility check deterministic:
        #
        # For each index i, let deadline_i(T) = latest occurrence of i within [1..T].
        # Then at any prefix t, the number of indices with deadline <= t must be <= t
        # (they can be placed in marking seconds within that prefix),
        # and additionally enough decrement time must remain:
        #   required total decrements <= T - (#indices that must be marked by time T)
        # But all indices are marked by time T, so marking count is exactly n:
        #   decrements <= T - n  (global)
        #
        # Wait: decrements can be interleaved; marking seconds are exactly n distinct seconds.
        # Therefore decrements available is exactly (T - n) regardless of which seconds are chosen.
        # Thus global constraint is simply:
        #   sum(need) <= T - n  <=> T >= n + sum(need)
        #
        # However, marking cannot occur at arbitrary times: each index i can only be marked
        # at one of its occurrences. That imposes additional deadline constraints.
        #
        # We reduce to:
        #   1) Global: total decrements fit.
        #   2) There exists an injective assignment of indices to occurrence times <= T.
        #
        # For injective assignment, deadlines from latest occurrences work:
        # Let for each i: d_i = latest pos <= T (exists since present).
        # Necessary and sufficient for assignment of n jobs with deadlines to n time slots
        # where each job needs 1 slot: sort deadlines and ensure j-th smallest deadline >= j
        # (classic scheduling with unit jobs).
        #
        # Combine with global decrement-time capacity: sum need <= T - n.
        total_need = sum(need)

        def feasible(T: int) -> bool:
            if T < n:  # need at least n seconds to place n markings
                return False
            if total_need > T - n:
                return False

            # compute latest occurrence <= T for each index
            latest = [0] * (n + 1)
            # Scan only up to T to keep it O(T) per check; T <= 2000 so OK.
            for t in range(1, T + 1):
                idx = changeIndices[t - 1]
                latest[idx] = t

            # all indices must have at least one occurrence by T
            for i in range(1, n + 1):
                if latest[i] == 0:
                    return False

            deadlines = sorted(latest[1:])  # length n
            # unit-time job scheduling: after sorting, for j=1..n, deadline[j-1] >= j
            for j, d in enumerate(deadlines, start=1):
                if d < j:
                    return False
            return True

        lo, hi = 1, m
        ans = -1
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = (lo + hi) // 2
            if feasible(mid):
                ans = mid
                hi = mid - 1
            else:
                lo = mid + 1
        return ans

from typing import List

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)

        # If an index never appears in changeIndices, it can never be marked.
        present = [False] * n
        for x in changeIndices:
            present[x - 1] = True
        if any(not present[i] for i in range(n)):
            return -1

        # Feasibility check for a fixed horizon T:
        # Let mark times for each index i be some occurrence <= T when we perform "mark".
        # At any second we can either decrement (consumes 1 decrement second) or mark (consumes 1 mark second).
        #
        # For a particular index i, we need nums[i] decrements before its chosen mark time.
        # If we choose to mark index i at time t, then we must have performed at least nums[i] decrements in
        # seconds [1..t-1].
        #
        # Across all indices, decrements share the same global second budget. Using a "latest possible"
        # marking assignment is optimal for feasibility: marking later increases available decrement
        # seconds in all relevant prefixes.
        #
        # Therefore, for fixed T, for each index i we take its latest occurrence time <= T, call it last[i].
        # Then each index must satisfy nums[i] decrements by time last[i]-1.
        #
        # Now sort required deadlines by the time last[i]. This reduces to verifying for every prefix:
        # total required decrements among those indices <= total available decrement seconds before
        # the corresponding marking times, accounting for mark seconds that also consume time.
        #
        # If k indices have latest mark times <= t, then within prefix [1..t]:
        # - k seconds are used for marks (one per index)
        # - the remaining t-k seconds can be used for decrements
        # So decrements used by those indices must fit in t-k.
        def feasible(T: int) -> bool:
            # collect (deadlineTime, requiredDecrements) for indices that must be marked
            # deadlineTime = last occurrence time <= T
            last = [-1] * n
            for s in range(T):  # seconds 1..T, 0-based index s
                idx = changeIndices[s] - 1
                last[idx] = s + 1  # overwrite => latest within T

            items = []
            for i in range(n):
                if last[i] == -1:
                    return False
                items.append((last[i], nums[i]))

            # Sort by latest occurrence time (deadline)
            items.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])

            decrements_required_prefix = 0
            for k in range(1, n + 1):
                t = items[k - 1][0]          # time of kth deadline in sorted order
                decrements_required_prefix += items[k - 1][1]
                # In prefix [1..t], we must have enough decrement seconds:
                # available = t - (#marks already scheduled in this prefix)
                # Since the first k items all have deadlines <= t, we can schedule their marks within [1..t],
                # consuming exactly k mark seconds within that prefix.
                available = t - k
                if decrements_required_prefix > available:
                    return False
            return True

        # Binary search for earliest feasible T in [1..m]
        lo, hi = 1, m
        ans = -1
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = (lo + hi) // 2
            if feasible(mid):
                ans = mid
                hi = mid - 1
            else:
                lo = mid + 1
        return ans

from typing import List

class Solution:
    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        m = len(changeIndices)
        if n == 0:
            return 0

        # Feasibility by time T:
        # Let occ[t] = index marked at second t if chosen. We can mark each index only at
        # its occurrences in [1..T] where its current value is 0.
        #
        # A key reduction:
        # For each i, nums[i] decrements must happen before the time we mark i.
        # Dec capacity is shared: in any prefix [1..t], the number of decrement-possible seconds
        # equals t - (#seconds where we spend doing marks).
        #
        # For a given T, the optimal choice is to mark each index i at its latest occurrence
        # within [1..T]. This only relaxes constraints for other indices because marking later
        # increases the available decrement window for everyone.
        #
        # Then feasibility becomes:
        # If index i's latest occurrence is at time pos[i], we need nums[i] decrements
        # in the prefix strictly before pos[i].
        # Considering the set of indices with pos <= t, the total required decrements
        # in their own windows cannot exceed the available decrement seconds in prefix [1..t],
        # which is: t - count_marked_so_far. With latest occurrences, count_marked_so_far
        # equals number of indices whose latest occurrence <= t (and all must be marked by T).
        #
        # So we need, for every prefix t (1..T):
        #   sum(nums[i] for indices i with pos[i] <= t) <= t - k
        # where k is that number of indices (k = count of i with pos[i] <= t).
        #
        # We can check this efficiently by sorting pos[i] and using prefix sums.

        # Precompute occurrences lists (1-indexed times).
        occ_times = [[] for _ in range(n + 1)]
        for t, idx in enumerate(changeIndices, start=1):
            occ_times[idx].append(t)

        # Quick impossibility: every index must appear in changeIndices at least once.
        for i in range(1, n + 1):
            if not occ_times[i]:
                return -1

        # Binary search earliest T in [1..m]
        # If T is infeasible, any smaller is infeasible; if T feasible, any larger is feasible.
        def feasible(T: int) -> bool:
            # For each i, determine its latest occurrence <= T.
            positions = []
            for i in range(1, n + 1):
                # latest occurrence <= T:
                # find rightmost <= T in occ_times[i]
                arr = occ_times[i]
                # binary search in arr
                lo, hi = 0, len(arr)
                while lo < hi:
                    mid = (lo + hi) // 2
                    if arr[mid] <= T:
                        lo = mid + 1
                    else:
                        hi = mid
                if lo == 0:
                    return False  # no occurrence within [1..T]
                pos = arr[lo - 1]
                positions.append((pos, nums[i - 1]))

            # Sort by pos ascending
            positions.sort()
            prefix_sum_req = 0
            k = 0
            # Check prefix constraints for t equal to each pos.
            # We can skip intermediate t because LHS only changes when we include a new index.
            for pos, req in positions:
                # For t = pos:
                # prefix required decrements includes all indices with pos' <= pos (i.e., k+1 after adding).
                prefix_sum_req += req
                k += 1
                # available decrement seconds in prefix [1..pos] = pos - k (because k seconds are used for marks)
                if prefix_sum_req > pos - k:
                    return False
            return True

        # Edge: if n>m it's still possibly feasible? Actually we need at least n seconds
        # to perform n marks, one per second, so must have m>=n. Constraint is n,m up to 2000,
        # but not explicitly m>=n. We can short-circuit.
        if m < n:
            return -1

        lo, hi = 1, m
        ans = -1
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = (lo + hi) // 2
            if feasible(mid):
                ans = mid
                hi = mid - 1
            else:
                lo = mid + 1
        return ans

    def earliestSecondToMarkIndices(self, nums: List[int], changeIndices: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
